stem: An object at initial temperature \( [[T0]]^\circ \) is placed in surroundings held at \( [[Ta]]^\circ \). By Newton's law of cooling its temperature after [[tau]] minutes is \( [[Ttau]]^\circ \). What is its temperature after [[t2]] minutes?
solution: Newton's law gives \( T(t) = [[Ta]] + ([[T0]] - [[Ta]])e^{-kt} \). The excess temperature is \( [[T0]] - [[Ta]] = [[D0]] \) initially and \( [[Ttau]] - [[Ta]] = [[D0]]/2 \) after [[tau]] minutes, so it halves each [[tau]] minutes. After [[t2]] minutes the excess is \( [[D0]]/4 \), giving \( T = [[Ta]] + \frac{([[Ttau]] - [[Ta]])^{2}}{[[T0]] - [[Ta]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the object reaches the surrounding temperature, as a linear drop would suggest.
This keeps the temperature at its value after the first interval, assuming no further cooling.
This applies a factor of \( 1/3 \) instead of the correct halving twice.
This uses an incorrect cooling factor.
This forgets any cooling and reports the initial temperature.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: An object obeys Newton's law of cooling \( \dfrac{dT}{dt} = -k(T - T_a) \) with \( k = [[k]] \) (per minute), ambient temperature \( T_a = [[Ta]]^\circ \), and initial temperature \( T(0) = [[T0]]^\circ \). What is \( T([[t]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This uses \( e^{+kt} \) instead of the decaying \( e^{-kt} \), giving unbounded growth.
This decays the full initial temperature to zero instead of decaying only the excess toward \( T_a \).
This ignores cooling entirely and reports the ambient temperature.
This flips the sign of the excess term.
This swaps the roles of the excess and the ambient temperature.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: An object cooling in a room of unknown, constant ambient temperature \( T_a \) is measured at three equally spaced times, giving readings \( [[T0]]^\circ \), then \( [[T1]]^\circ \), then \( [[T2]]^\circ \). Using Newton's law of cooling, what is the ambient temperature \( T_a \)?
solution: Under Newton's law the excess temperature decays geometrically, so consecutive excesses satisfy \( \dfrac{T_1 - T_a}{T_0 - T_a} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_a}{T_1 - T_a} \), i.e. \( (T_1 - T_a)^{2} = (T_0 - T_a)(T_2 - T_a) \). The quadratic terms in \( T_a \) cancel, leaving a linear equation whose solution is \( T_a = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This averages the three readings, which is not valid for a geometric (exponential) decay.
This assumes the object has already reached equilibrium at the last reading.
This averages only the first and last readings, ignoring the geometric nature of the decay.
This linearly extrapolates the trend instead of using the geometric-ratio relation.
This reuses the middle reading, ignoring the ambient temperature entirely.
This is one more than the correct ambient temperature.
This is one less than the correct ambient temperature.

stem: Two objects sit in the same room, held at a constant ambient temperature \( [[Ta]]^\circ \); each obeys Newton's law of cooling with the same rate constant \( k \). Object A cools from \( [[TA0]]^\circ \) to \( [[TA1]]^\circ \) over some time interval. If object B starts at \( [[TB0]]^\circ \), what is its temperature after that same time interval?
solution: Both objects share the same decay factor \( e^{-k\Delta t} = \dfrac{T_{A1} - T_a}{T_{A0} - T_a} \), since they are in the same room. Applying that ratio to object B: \( T_{B1} = T_a + (T_{B0} - T_a)\cdot\dfrac{T_{A1} - T_a}{T_{A0} - T_a} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts object A's absolute temperature drop from B's start instead of using the proportional excess ratio.
This inverts the ratio, using A's excess ratio upside down.
This assumes object B does not cool at all.
This assumes object B ends up at the same temperature object A reached.
This applies A's ratio backward, dividing by the wrong excess.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
